content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 21, 2001

Sam Perrotti
Assistant Commissioner - Subdivisions
Department of Real Estate
Post Office Box 187005
Sacramento, CA 95818-7005

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-01-110

Dear Mr. Perrotti:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your question concerns the doctrine of "Incompatible
Activities," which is not a provision of the Act. Though we cannot render advice in your
case, we are providing general information concerning the Act. (Reg. 18329(c).)

The Fair Political Practices Commission advises only on the requirements of the
Political Reform Act. The doctrine of "Incompatible Activities" is under the jurisdiction
of the Attorney General's office.

FACTS

You have been employed with the State of California, Department of Real Estate
since 1976. In August 1995, you were appointed a planning commissioner with the City
of Hermosa Beach. In December 2000, you were promoted in the Department of Real
Estate to the position of assistant commissioner, subdivisions.

The planning commission implements the Subdivision Map Act, Government
Code Sections 65000 to 66025. The Department of Real Estate implements the
Subdivision Lands Act, Business and Professions Code Sections 10000 to 11200.

The Department of Real Estate has jurisdiction over subdivisions with five or
more interests in land. Because the City of Hermosa Beach is almost completely

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. I-01-110
Page No. 2

developed, its planning commission rarely reviews developments with five or more
interests in land.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit public officials from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use their official position to
influence governmental decisions in which they have a financial interest. (Section
87100.) The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for determining
whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest under Regulation 18700,
subdivisions (b)(1)-(8):

"(1) Determine whether the individual is a public
official, within the meaning of the Act....

"(2) Determine whether the public official will be
making, participating in making, or using or attempting to
use his/her official position to influence a government
decision....

"(3) Identify the public official's economic
interests....

"(4) For each of the public official's economic
interests, determine whether that interest is directly or
indirectly involved in the governmental decision....

"(5) Determine the applicable materiality standard
for each economic interest....

"(6) Determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have a material financial effect ... on each
economic interest identified....

"(7) Determine if the reasonably foreseeable
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally....

"(8) Determine if the public official's participation
is legally required despite the conflict of interest...."

An official is prohibited from participating in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on any source of income
aggregating five hundred dollars or more within a 12-month period (Section 87103(c).)
In this context, it would appear that your salary from the Department of Real Estate

File No. I-01-110
Page No. 3

would be deemed a source of income to you. However, for purposes of the Act, section
82030(b)(2) provides that "income" does not include "salary and reimbursement of
expenses or per diem received from a state, local or federal government agency."
Therefore, by operation of the "government salary exception," the salary and
employment-related benefits received through your position as assistant commissioner
are not sources of income to you as defined by the Act, and you would not need to
disqualify yourself from decisions on the planning commission that are under the
jurisdiction of your employer.

However, you would have a second and distinct economic interest which may
give rise to a conflict of interest as a planning commissioner, if a particular decision had
an economic interest, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on your own
personal finances (i.e., personal expenses, assets or liabilities.) (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703 et seq.) Should these circumstances arise, we suggest you to submit
the situation-specific facts to the Commission for a determination of your voting
eligibility.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Joan Giannetta
Secretary, Legal Division

/jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\01-110

Code Sections 65000 to 66025. The Department of Real Estate implements the

The Department of Real Estate has jurisdiction over subdivisions with five or
more interests in land. Because the City of Hermosa Beach is almost completely
developed, its planning commission rarely reviews developments with five or more
interests in land.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether an official who works as an Assistant Commissioner for the Department of Real Estate can participate in planning commission decisions regarding subdivisions, and whether conflicts of interest arise from holding both positions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under the Political Reform Act's 'government salary exception,' the official's state employment salary is not a source of income for conflict purposes, so they do not need to disqualify themselves from planning decisions based on their employment. However, conflicts may arise if planning decisions have material financial effects on the official's personal finances, requiring situation-specific submission for eligibility determinations.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains clear Facts and Analysis sections but no separate QUESTION or CONCLUSION/SHORT ANSWER headers. The informal advice letter includes regulatory analysis and an eight-step conflict of interest test. Minor OCR errors present ('Polrrrcar', 'Pnrcrrcrs') but content is legible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether an official who works as an Assistant Commissioner for the Department of Real Estate can participate in planning commission decisions regarding subdivisions, and whether conflicts of interest arise from holding both positions.

CONCLUSION: Under the Political Reform Act's 'government salary exception,' the official's state employment salary is not a source of income for conflict purposes, so they do not need to disqualify themselves from planning decisions based on their employment. However, conflicts may arise if planning decisions have material financial effects on the official's personal finances, requiring situation-specific submission for eligibility determinations.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides informal advice to a Department of Real Estate Assistant Commissioner regarding whether he must disqualify himself from planning commission decisions due to potential conflicts of interest. The FPPC concludes that the government salary exception exempts employment-related income from conflict rules, but personal financial interests may still require recusal in specific situations.
